coredata.dc:title: A novel sandwiched electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on carbon quantum dots and signal amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Nian-Lu
Jia, Li-Ping
Ma, Rong-Na
Jia, Wen-Li
Lu, Yi-Yang
Shi, Sha-Shan
Wang, Huai-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel sandwiched electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was developed. The nanocomposite of polydopamine and Ag nanoparticles (PDA-AgNPs) was prepared by the redox reaction between Ag+ and dopamine. This nanocomposite not only provided an effective matrix for the immobilization of primary antibody (Ab1) but also enhanced the conductivity of the electrode. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were immobilized on the poly(ethylenimine) functionalized graphene oxide (PEI-GO) through amido-bond. Then Au nanoparticles were decorated on the CQDs modified PEI-GO matrix, and the resulted complex AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO was introduced to link secondary antibody (Ab2). The CQDs can be connected to the electrode surface through the combination of CEA with Ab1 and Ab2, and then the amplified electrochemiluminescence signal of CQDs was obtained with the synergistic effect of AgNPs, polydopamine, AuNPs and PEI-GO. Under the optimal conditions, the ECL intensity was proportional to the logarithm value of CEA concentration in the linear range from 5pgmL−1 to 500ngmL−1 with a detection limit of 1.67pgmL−1 for CEA detection. The immunosensor was applied for the CEA detection in real samples with satisfactory results. The proposed ECL immunosensor showed good performance with high sensitivity, specificity, reproducibility, stability and will be potential in clinical detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence immunosensor
Carcinoembryonic antigen
Carbon quantum dots
Functionalized graphene oxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PEI functionalized graphene oxide
Immobilization of Ab2 on the AuNPs /CQDs-PEI-GO matrix
ECL detection of the prepared immunosensor
Characterization of CQDs, GO, AuNPs and AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO
Detection of CEA with the immunosensor
Stability, reproducibility and specificity of the immunosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375055, 21405070, 21505063 and 21427808).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CQDs (A), GO (B), AuNPs (C) and AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO (D). Inset of (D) is the partial enlarged picture.

(A) UV–vis spectra of AuNPs, CQDs and GO. (B) FL emission and excitation spectra of the CQDs. (C) FL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths. Inset showed the relationship between FL emission intensity and excitation wavelength.
(A) ECL intensity-potential curves of CQDs/GCE. (B) Stability of the ECL signals of CQDs/GCE Scanning from −1.8 to −0.2V in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8..

EIS of the different electrodes: (a) bare GCE (b) PDA-AgNPs/GCE (c) Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE, (d) BSA/Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE, (e) CEA/BSA/Ab1/PDA-AgNPs /GCE, (f)AuNPs/CQDs-GO-PEI/Ab2/CEA/BSA/Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE in 0.1molL−1 KCl containing 5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The frequency range is between 0.01 and 100,000Hz with amplitude of 5mV.

(A) The ECL intensity of the prepared immunosensor in different pH values of PBS. (B) The ECL intensity of the immunosensor at different incubation time of CEA. (C) The ECL intensity at different incubation temperature of CEA.
ECL signals of the immunosensor for the detection of CEA at different concentrations (a→g: 0.005, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100, 500ngmL−1). (B) The calibration plot between ECL intensity and logc CEA.
The stability of the proposed ECL immunosensor under consecutive cyclic potential scans for 20 cycles.
(A) The fabrication process of proposed ECL immunosensor. (B) The immobilization of AuNPs, CQDs and Ab2 on the PEI-GO matrix.
Comparison of different methods for CEA determination.
Detection of CEA in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: A novel sandwiched electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on carbon quantum dots and signal amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252059, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel sandwiched electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was developed. The nanocomposite of polydopamine and Ag nanoparticles (PDA-AgNPs) was prepared by the redox reaction between Ag+ and dopamine. This nanocomposite not only provided an effective matrix for the immobilization of primary antibody (Ab1) but also enhanced the conductivity of the electrode. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were immobilized on the poly(ethylenimine) functionalized graphene oxide (PEI-GO) through amido-bond. Then Au nanoparticles were decorated on the CQDs modified PEI-GO matrix, and the resulted complex AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO was introduced to link secondary antibody (Ab2). The CQDs can be connected to the electrode surface through the combination of CEA with Ab1 and Ab2, and then the amplified electrochemiluminescence signal of CQDs was obtained with the synergistic effect of AgNPs, polydopamine, AuNPs and PEI-GO. Under the optimal conditions, the ECL intensity was proportional to the logarithm value of CEA concentration in the linear range from 5pgmL−1 to 500ngmL−1 with a detection limit of 1.67pgmL−1 for CEA detection. The immunosensor was applied for the CEA detection in real samples with satisfactory results. The proposed ECL immunosensor showed good performance with high sensitivity, specificity, reproducibility, stability and will be potential in clinical detection.

ce:section.ce:para: As well known to all, cancer is a kind of complex disease which greatly threatens the human health in recent years. Tumor markers are biomacromolecules which are associated with the cancer whose measurement or identification is important in diagnosis and prognosis (Kiang et al., 1990; Schneider et al., 2000; Ilantzis et al., 2002; Lin et al., 2014). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is one kind of glycoprotein most often associated with colorectal carcinomas and is widely used as a tumor marker for clinical diagnosis of liver, colon, breast and colorectal cancer (Duffy, 2001; Huang et al., 2010; Iwazawa et al., 2000; Naghibalhossaini and Ebadi, 2006). So the sensitive detection of CEA can provide essential information in early monitoring and screening disease recurrence (Kulasingam and Diamandis, 2008; Sun et al., 2013).

Up to now, a number of immunoassay techniques for the detection of CEA have been developed including fluoroimmunoassay, enzyme immunoassay, electrochemical immunoassay, radioimmunoassay, and chemiluminescence assay (Yuan et al., 2007; Wang, 2006; Tothill, 2009; Suni, 2008; Jacobs et al., 2010; Yuan et al., 2001; Lai et al., 2011). Among these methods, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay has gained increasing attention due to the advantages such as high sensitivity, good selectivity, low background signal and rapid response (Mao et al., 2011; Zhuo et al., 2014; Blackburn et al., 1991). For the development of sandwich-type ECL immunosensor, the immobilization of labeled secondary antibodies (Ab2) is a vital step for generating signals to improve the sensitivity of the immunosensor (Lai et al., 2011). So introducing abundant signal amplification elements in favor of the sensitive detection of CEA is the main goal.

Luminol, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), Ru(bpy)3 2+and their analogues have been widely investigated as ECL-luminophores (Wang et al., 2012a; Yang et al., 2014; Xiao et al., 2013). However, these luminescence species have some disadvantages. For example, Ru(bpy)3 2+is difficult to be immobilized on the electrode surface modified with biomolecules due to the lack of functional groups on its structure. Luminol can generate strong ECL signal in alkaline solution. But its ECL signal is very weak in neutral solution, which limits its application in the biological system (Qi, 2004). Compared with Ru(bpy)3 2+ and luminol, QDs have been widely used in immunosensors attributed to their unique properties such as controllability, biocompatibility, low toxicity and large modifiable specific surface area (Fang et al., 2012; Jie et al., 2013). Among those substances, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a kind of environmentally friendly material compared with the metal semiconductor QDs such as CdSe and CdTe. Recently CQDs have become a hot topic in carbon nanomaterial research (Sun et al., 2006) due to its unique optical properties of high photostability, high surface-to-volume reaction, high electrical conductivity and tunable excitation, as well as low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Furthermore, there are amounts of functionalized carboxyl groups on its surface so that it can be easily labeled for ECL detection (Qiao et al., 2010). Based on the quenching effect of the analytes on the ECL of CQDs, the CQDs were used to detect many analytes such as Cd2+(Li et al., 2012), Cu2+(Xu et al., 2013), and S2− (Wang et al., 2014). Moreover, the CQDs were employed as labeling agents for immunoassays of cancer markers (Wang et al., 2012b; Zhang et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2015), cells (Wu et al., 2013) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) (Lu et al., 2013).

Graphene is a single layer of aromatic carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb two-dimensional (2D) lattice (Novoselov et al., 2004; Geim et al., 2007). Owing to its unique structure, graphene has shown some novel properties such as good mechanical strength, large specific surface area, and outstanding electric conductivity (Xia et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2011). However, its availability in electrochemistry is encumbered by the high cohesive van der Waals energy adhering graphitic sheets to one another. So the surface modification of graphene is an essential step for obtaining a molecular level dispersion of individual graphene. In this assay, we prepared poly(ethylenimine) functionalized graphene oxide (PEI-GO). The existence of PEI on graphene oxide not only provided water solubility but also endowed the nanocomposite with -NH2 group, which is favorable to immobilize other CQDs and AuNPs.

In the present work, a sensitive sandwich-type ECL immunosensor was successfully designed for the detection of CEA. The ECL immunosensor was prepared using the nanocomposite of polydopamine and Ag nanoparticles (PDA-AgNPs) as platform to immobilize primary antibody (Ab1). And the complex of poly(ethylenimine) functionalized graphene oxide (PEI-GO) modified with CQDs and AuNPs was used to immobilize secondary antibody (Ab2). The ECL of CQDs can be obtained with the combination of CEA with Ab1 and Ab2. The ECL signals of CQDs were greatly amplified under the synergistic effect of AgNPs, polydopamine, AuNPs and PEI-GO, which served as favorable conductive materials to promote electron transfer rate and further improved electrochemical sensing ability.

Human colon cancer carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and carcinoembryonic antigen antibody (anti-CEA) were purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HAuCl4·4H2O, bovine serum albumin (BSA), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. l-lysine, Poly (ethylenimine) (PEI) and dopamine hydrochloride (DA·HCl) were purchased from Aladdin Industry Corporation. Phosphate buffered solutions (PBS, pH 7.4) were prepared using 0.1molL−1 Na2HPO4 and 0.1molL−1 KH2PO4. 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 containing 0.1molL−1 KCl was used as the ECL electrolyte. All chemical reagents were analytical grade. Double distilled water was used through the whole study. Human serum samples were provided by Liaocheng People’s Hospital.

Electrochemiluminescence measurements were carried out on a Model RFL-1 ECL analyzer (Xi’an Remex Instrument Co., Ltd., China) with the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) set at 800V and with the auxiliary equipment of CHI 760C (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., China) electrochemical workstation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using a CHI 760C electrochemical workstation. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at 200kV (JEOL Ltd., Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained with a LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA).

CQDs were prepared as follows: l-lysine (0.2g) was dissolved in the mixed solution of ethylene glycol (5mL) and ultrapure water (10mL) under ultrasonication. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. The obtained brown-yellow product was placed in a dialysis bag (MW=3500) and then the solution out of the dialysis bag was vacuum evaporated in a rotary evaporator at 45°C to obtain the target CQDs. The resulting solution was stored at 4°C for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PEI functionalized graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared according to Hummer's method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958). Then 0.3g as-synthesized GO was dispersed in 200mL of double distilled water under ultrasonication for 30min, subsequently 0.1g PEI was added. The mixed solution was stirred at 60°C for 12h. After being cooled to room temperature, the resulting dispersion was centrifuged, washed and dried. The resultant product of PEI-GO was redispersed in water for further use (Kim et al., 2012).

AuNPs were prepared according to the previously reported literature (Handley, 1989). Briefly, 2mL of 50mmolL−1 HAuCl4 was added into 98mL of water and then refluxed with stirring.When the solution was heated to boiling, 10mL of 38.8mmolL−1 sodium citrate was quickly added. When the color changed from pale yellow to deep red, the solution was stirred for another 20min. Then, the solution was translated into brown volumetric flask after being cooled to room temperature and then stored at 4°C for further use.

25mL CQDs solution was added into the PEI-GO solution and stirred for 3h at 80°C. The obtained product of CQDs-PEI-GO was centrifuged and dispersed in water again. 0.1mL EDC and NHS (4:1) mixed solution was added into 10mL CQDs-PEI-GO solution with stirring for 30min, subsequently 5mL AuNPs was added and the mixture was kept stirring for 6h. The resulting product AuNPs /CQDs-PEI-GO was centrifuged and dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of Ab2 on the AuNPs /CQDs-PEI-GO matrix
ce:section.ce:para: 20μL EDC and NHS (4:1) mixed solution were added into 1mL of AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO solution and stirred for 5min. Then 200μL Ab2 (50μgmL−1) was added and kept shaking for another 5min. Finally 20μL BSA was added into the above solution to block the active sites. The resultant solution was centrifuged after being placed over night at 4°C and then was dispersed in 1mL PBS (pH 7.4).

A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was successively polished with 0.3 and 0.05µm alumina slurry. Following that, the GCE was ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and double distilled water and dried with nitrogen. The fabrication process of the ECL immunosensor was shown in Scheme 1. Firstly, the PDA-AgNPs nanocomposites modified electrode (PDA-AgNPs/GCE) was prepared by dropping 4μL DA (0.1mgmL−1, 10mmolL−1 Tris, pH 8.5) and 4μL AgNO3 (2mgmL−1) on the electrode surface. After drying and washing, 5μL anti-CEA (Ab1) solution (20μgmL−1) was dropped onto the electrode surface and then, the electrode was incubated for 12h at 4°C to get Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE. Subsequently, 5μL BSA (1%) solution was dropped on the Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE and incubated for 40min at 37.5°C to block the non-specific binding sites. Following that, the BSA/Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE was incubated in different concentrations of CEA solution at 37.5°C for 40min. Finally, 5μL Ab2/CQDs-PEI- GO/AuNPs solution was dropped on the CEA/BSA/Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE for immunoreaction at 37.5°C for 1h. The obtained sandwich-type ECL immunosensor was stored at 4°C when not in use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL detection of the prepared immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL measurements were performed in a detector cell containing 5mL PBS (0.1molL−1 pH 7.4) with 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 and 0.1molL−1 KCl. In the ECL immunoassay, an Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum wire were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode respectively, and the AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO/Ab2/CEA/BSA/Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE was used as working electrode. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was maintained at 800V and the potential range was from −1.8V to −0.2V with a scan rate of 200mVs−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, GO, AuNPs and AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 showed TEM images of CQDs, GO, AuNPs and AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO. It can be seen that the CQDs are uniform spherical shapes with average size of about 4–5nm (Fig. 1A). Graphene oxide nanosheets illustrated typical 2D flake-like wrinkled shapes with irregular size (Fig. 1B). And AuNPs showed uniform spherical shapes with the diameter of about 13nm (Fig. 1C). The morphology of the AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO was shown in Fig. 1D. It can be seen that there are different sized particles loaded on the thin layer. The thin layer different from the image of GO (Fig. 1B) indicated the PEI functionalized graphene oxide (PEI-GO) was obtained and the different sized particles (Inset of Fig. 1D) demonstrated both CQDs and AuNPs were successfully decorated on the surface of PEI-GO.

The optical properties of CQDs, GO and AuNPs were characterized with UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 2A, GO showed a strong absorption peak at 235nm, which was assigned to the π-π* transition of the benzenoid rings. The typical absorption peaks of AuNPs were observed at 245nm and 520nm respectively. The as-prepared CQDs exhibited a strong absorption peak at around 280nm, which was attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. Fig. 2B showed the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs. There are two excitation peaks appeared around at 248 and 368nm. When CQDs were excited at 365nm, a maximum emission peak at 443nm was observed. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 2C, CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission. The emission wavelength of CQDs was gradually red-shifted when the excitation wavelength changed from 280nm to 460nm. However, the emission intensity increased from 280nm to 360nm and then decreased with the excitation wavelength changing from 360nm to 460nm (Inset of Fig. 2C). The results were in agreement with that reported previously (Yang et al., 2012; Chowdhury et al., 2012).

Fig. 3A displayed ECL-potential curves of CQDs/GCE scanning from −1.8 to −0.2V in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8. The ECL response was about at −1.0V. Fig. 3B presented a good ECL stability of CQDs/GCE under continuous 18 cycles of CV sweeps. According to the reported literatures (Zheng et al., 2009; Dong et al., 2010), the ECL mechanism may be illustrated as follows:
(1)
CQDs + e
−
→
CQDs
−
∙
(2)
S
2
O
8
2 −
+ e
−
→
S
2

8
2 −
∙
(3)
S
2

8
2 −
∙ →
SO
4
2 −
+ SO
4
−
∙
(4)
CQDs
−
∙
+ SO
4
−
∙ →
CQDs * + SO
4
2 −
(5)
CQDs * → CQDs + h v

As control tests, the electrochemical characteristics of differently modified electrodes were investigated after each step by using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-couple was employed as the electrochemical redox probe for EIS measurements, while the Nyquist plots were utilized to calculate the electron-transfer resistance. EIS curves for differently modified electrode were exhibited in Fig. 4. As shown in Fig. 4, a very small semicircle was observed at bare GCE, indicating a low electron transfer resistance. When PDA-AgNPs was covered on the GCE surface, a higher resistance was obtained which maybe caused by the two factors: (1) PDA is an organic semiconductor which will dramatically hinder the electron transfer from the redox probe of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− to the electrode surface; (McGinness et al., 1974). (2) the repulsion between Fe(CN)6 3-/4-and negatively charged AgNPs will resulted in high resistance. When Ab1, BSA, CEA was modified layer by layer on the electrode, continuously increasing resistance were obtained (curve c-e), which indicated the successful modification of these non-conductive bioactive materials. After AuNPs/CQDs-PEI-GO/Ab2 was linked to the electrode, the resistance decreased compared with the CEA/BSA/Ab1/PDA-AgNPs/GCE, which was attributed to the high conductivity of AuNPs.

In order to enhance the sensitivity of the immunosensor, the experimental conditions have been studied in detail. The pH value of the detection solution has a great effect on the electrochemical behavior of the immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 5A, the ECL intensity of the immunosensor first increased and then decreased with the pH ranging from 5.0 to 9.0, and the maximal value of the ECL intensity was achieved at pH 7.4 (Fig. 5A). Herein, the detection of CEA was performed in pH 7.4 PBS. The incubation time of CEA is an important parameter affecting the analytical performance of the immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 5B, the ECL signals increased with an increasing incubation time from 15min to 40min and then decreased when the incubation time was more than 40min. Thus, the incubation time of 40min was selected for the subsequent experiments. Furthermore, the effect of incubation temperature is important to the activity of the antibody and antigen. To obtain optimal ECL signals, the incubation temperature of CEA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5C, the ECL intensity was maximal when the incubation temperature was at 37.5°C. Thus, 37.5°C was selected for the sandwich-type immunodevice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CEA with the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, the designed ECL immunosensor was carried out to detect the concentrations of target CEA. As shown in Fig. 6, the ECL intensity increased linearly with the logarithm value of CEA concentration over the range from 5pgmL−1 to 500ngmL−1. The regression equation was I ECL=3380.3+1138.4 lgc CEA/ng mL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9989. The detection limit for CEA was 1.67pgmL−1 (S/N=3). The result suggested that the ECL immunosensor could be utilized to quantitatively detect CEA antigen. In addition, as shown in Table 1, the present work for CEA detection based on the sandwiched ECL immunosensor showed a lower detection limit and wider linear range than some reported methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and specificity of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility and stability were the major concerns for practical application of immunosensors. The reproducibility of the as-prepared immunosensor was investigated by prepared seven parallel electrodes under the same conditions. And the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 6.7% for the same concentration of CEA (10ngmL−1). Fig. 7 showed the ECL signals of the immunosensor under continuously cyclic potential scanning for 20 cycles in 5mL PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8. It can be seen that the ECL intensity did not show an obvious change, and the RSD of the ECL intensity was 0.5%. When the immunosensor was stored at 4°C over 30 days, the ECL signal of the immunosensor for the CEA detection decreased by 9.3%. In short, the immunosensor had good stability and reproducibility.

The specificity of the immunosensor played an important role in analyzing biological samples in situ without separation. To investigate the selectivity of the immunosensor, some substances such as cholesterol, uric acid, glucose and human serum albumin which exist in human blood were used as the interference to evaluate the specificity. The mixture containing 0.51mgmL−1 of cholesterol, 0.6mgmL−1 of uric acid, 20mgmL−1 of glucose, 600mgmL−1 of human serum albumin and 5ngmL−1 of CEA was detected by the prepared immunosensor. The ECL intensity in the presence of interference decreased 8.7% compared with that without the interference, indicating the selectivity of the proposed immunosensor was acceptable.

In order to monitor the feasibility of the ECL immunosensor, human serum samples from different people were analyzed using the proposed immunosensor. As shown in Table 2, the concentrations of CEA in these samples were obtained and compared with those obtained by ELISA. The relative errors were less than 10%, which was acceptable and satisfied. Hence, the developed immunosensor provided a promising tool for the detection of CEA in real samples.

In this study, a novel sensitive sandwiched ECL immunosensor based on CQDs for the detection of CEA was developed. Firstly, CQDs was chosen to be the testing target due to its traits of extremely low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. And then PEI-GO and AuNPs were selected to label Ab2 attributed to their features of fast electron transportation, high specific surface area and good biocompatibility. Combining the advantages of CQDs, AgNPs, PEI-GO and AuNPs, the ECL immunosensor can be named a green immunosensor. The immunosensor had the capacity to determine CEA from 5pgmL−1 to 500ngmL−1 with the detection limit of 1.67pgmL−1. Meanwhile, the immunosensor was successfully used in the determination of CEA in human serum. The results suggested the proposed ECL immunosensor presented good stability, acceptable reproducibility and specificity. Hence, the immunosensor provides a promising potential for clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375055, 21405070, 21505063 and 21427808).
